data.title: SGDM: Gold's Best Year Since 1979, Expecting Momentum To Continue
data.description:
Gold is up 46% year-to-date. That’s the best performance since 1979, and investors in gold-mining companies have scored incredible gains in 2025. Believe it or not, the Sprott Gold Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:SGDM) is now up 453%, dividends included, over the past 10 years. That trounces gold’s 247% gain.

I was bullish on SGDM back in the second quarter, and I reiterated a "Buy" rating. I expect further upside into year-end, perhaps on a chase-for-performance trading situation among global portfolio managers. Shares are up a stunning 43% since my early-June analysis.

Gold's Best Year Since 1979

According to the issuer, SGDM "seeks investment results that correspond generally to the performance of its underlying index, the Solactive Gold Miners Custom Factors Index. The Index aims to track the performance of larger-sized gold companies whose stocks are listed on Canadian and major U.S. exchanges." The Index uses a rules-based methodology designed to emphasize larger-sized gold companies with the highest revenue growth, free cash flow yield, and the lowest long-term debt to equity.

SGDM remains a small ETF with just $603 million in assets under management as of Oct. 1, 2025. That’s up from $387 million at the time of my previous assessment.

Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 50 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is low at only 0.46%, which is about two percentage points below that of the global ex-US stock market (86% of SGDM is non-US, which has benefited from a 10% YTD dollar drop).

Share-price momentum is incredibly high, as you might expect. The ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system is a pristine A+. Still, SDGM can be a risky product, and sharp drawdowns can occur with little warning.

Finally, on liquidity, the fund has mixed indicators, given average daily volume of more than 55,000 shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of 30 basis points, per Sprott ETFs. Thus, I encourage prospective investors to use limit orders, particularly around the market open.

For an update on the portfolio, the 3-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar plots on the far right side of the style box, indicating its bent to the global growth style. At the same time, the price-to-earnings ratio is low at only 14.5x. Pair that with a very high long-term earnings growth rate, and I just can’t justify downgrading the fund to a hold today.

SGDM: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

SGDM is a 100% Materials-sector product. In fact, the top 10 holdings account for a high 64% of the allocation. So, monitoring both the fundamentals and technicals of the top holdings is important, particularly as the Q3 earnings season gets underway in two weeks.

I also suspect that we may see a rise in dividend payments over the quarters ahead, given the high level of free cash flow being generated by gold-mining companies.

SGDM: Holdings & Dividend Information.

Seasonally, SGDM should have traded lower during August and September, as those are the two weakest back-to-back months on the calendar. That didn’t happen, which is exceptionally bullish, in my view.

When price does not follow the seasonal script, attention is warranted. So, with better (but still not stellar) calendar trends through year-end, I think the right call is to stay long.

SGDM: Shares Bucked the Usual Bearish Aug-Sept Trend

The Technical Take

Amid the meteoric rise so far in 2025, SGDM’s technical situation is strong. Notice in the chart below that shares are near all-time highs, while the RSI momentum oscillator has been firmly in high territory since the middle of the third quarter. This brings up an important point in terms of how to interpret technical indicators—simply because the RSI is above 70, it doesn’t mean that a breakdown in price is imminent. Rather, technical traders would rather see durable price strength than protracted weakness.

As it stands, SGMD has support way down at $47 (the fund blew through my low-$50s technical target that I detailed in early June). Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s on the rise, confirming that the bulls control the primary trend. All the while, an uptrend support line is in play down at $51, slightly below the 50dma. Could we see a pullback? Sure, but fighting the trend today may not be the probabilistic move.

I’d also call out how high dollar volume is right now. While the volume bars at the bottom of the graph are not big, they are significant because the fund price is much higher today compared to, say, two years ago.

SGDM: Bullish Uptrend, Extended vs the 200dma, Strong RSI, Rising Dollar Volume

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on SGDM. Up 126% YTD, I expect more gains into the end of the year. A performance chase could take the product to even higher levels.

data.title: SMH: AI Bubble? Chip Stocks Are Rewriting History
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The ‘AI Bubble’ has become the new face of the boundary line that demarcates bullish from bearish territories in the stock market today.

Recent fears about the AI bubble popping started from this private dinner involving OpenAI’s Sam Altman and have now snowballed into the billion-dollar question with traces from the dot-com bubble, as Fortune asks aloud, “Are we watching history repeat itself?”

Markets have shrugged off concerns so far, with the S&P 500 closing at ATHs. And so has VanEck Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SMH). Interestingly, despite concerns of the AI bubble popping, despite markets hitting ATH, and despite VanEck’s Semiconductor ETF hitting an ATH while still outperforming the market, there are some solid reasons why I continue to remain bullish on semiconductor stocks.

Semiconductor companies are reporting one of the best rates in sales inventory levels, signaling sustained momentum in elevated demand, which is beneficial for SMH.

This is just one of the reasons that I allude to below for the reasons why I reiterate my Buy recommendation on SMH.

SMH’s Fund Composition Continues To Evolve Towards Heavy Equipment

Before I contrast the concerns about the AI bubble versus the strong set of fundamental catalysts that continue to be in place for all the chip stocks, I will add some critical housekeeping updates about SMH, which I think are crucial for investors to know.

When I last wrote about SMH, I noted how the composition of the fund had changed with increasing exposure towards memory chip makers and the semiconductor equipment companies that support the complex supply chain of the semiconductor ecosystem.

I’ve added a chart below that shows how much more exposure SMH investors are getting towards the semiconductor equipment companies like ASML Holding N.V. (ASML), Lam Research Corporation (LRCX), Applied Materials, Inc. (AMAT), etc., and memory chip maker Micron Technology, Inc. (MU). Also, note the big bump that investors have gotten towards Intel Corporation (INTC) via SMH.

(On a slightly separate note, I think SMH did well to allocate investor capital towards Intel in the last quarter. Investors can read my latest coverage on Intel).

Obviously, SMH is making these changes in its fund allocation because the underlying index that it tracks, the MVIS US Listed Semiconductor 25 Index, is making those changes to its index.

But the changes are noteworthy and follow the same theme that I am observing in my previous coverage on SMH — that the AI buildout is broadening and the list of beneficiaries in the semiconductor ecosystem is expanding upstream towards key chip component makers and equipment suppliers like the names that I mentioned a couple of paragraphs ago. In my view this is only happening because AI catalysts are pushing big names in the fabless semi arena like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), and Broadcom Inc. (AVGO).

Semiconductor Companies Are Selling Out Like Never Before

I have a deep obsession with watching the inventories of semiconductor companies. It is, in my humble opinion, the source of truth that actually unites bulls and bears in their quest for truth, i.e., is there an AI bubble that is ready to burst?

Why is that? Because most semiconductor companies are cyclical. And it is important that semiconductor companies manage their raw materials and inventories accordingly to match the cyclical nature of their business. Otherwise they risk holding too much inventory if business turns south, and that eventually affects gross margins and cash flows.

Having said that, I pulled the dollar inventory volume of each semiconductor company that is part of the 26 names in the SMH portfolio and compared it against the revenues of the same companies. What I observed is that the cumulative inventory levels of all 26 names currently held in SMH when compared to the cumulative revenues of the same names are falling.

I even stripped out Nvidia from all SMH holdings, just because Nvidia has been the biggest beneficiary of the AI boom, and I still see that the inventory levels versus revenues of all other SMH holdings have dropped significantly to 15.8% for all holdings and to 18% for all holdings versus Nvidia. Per my observation, the current set of SMH holdings has never enjoyed such a rapid drawdown in cumulative inventories in the past two years as compared to the years since 2015.

Falling inventories as a percent of revenues as seen from FY23 until the latest TTM numbers in the recent reports of all companies showcase one hard truth — that semi companies are growing their revenues faster than the rate of inventories and raw materials held at their factories and warehouses. This is great news for gross margins for all SMH holdings because this vociferously means demand is very strong for semiconductors.

But I do want to point out something exclusively for the SMH fund. As an ETF, SMH stands to benefit more from this exploding demand for chips and semiconductor components because of its unique allocation, which is heavily weighted towards Nvidia on one hand, and the increasing exposure towards equipment makers. Investors can see how this has helped in the SMH fund, giving meaningful alpha vs. its peers, as seen below.

Valuation and Outlook For SMH

When I look at SMH, I see that it still trades at an attractive valuation of ~26x earnings. This valuation multiple has slightly reduced from the earnings multiple of 26.6 when I last observed. More importantly, I believe that SMH’s valuation looks very attractive when one sees that the long-term earnings CAGR is in the mid-teens range.

In my view, SMH’s current setup from a valuation standpoint is still very attractive, allowing investors to buy in at current levels.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

In the Fortune report that I linked to at the start of this article, the author of the report opined on a very important aspect of the AI economy when analyzing the bull/bear thesis for the AI bubble. The report noted that parallels between today’s AI bubble narrative and the 1990s dot-com bubble “are unmistakable” because of the “AI infrastructure buildout.”

But the author noted a crucial point that “unlike many dot-com companies that had no revenue, major AI players are generating substantial income.” Every AI player who is making revenue is taking a portion of that revenue to invest it further upstream, making semiconductor players in the chip ecosystem strong beneficiaries. As of now, I do not see any signs of that demand slowing down, as noted by the inventory levels of semiconductor companies above.

Should demand dry up for the “AI infrastructure buildout,” investors can expect severe headwinds impacting key semiconductor names in SMH.

One last point about seasonality. We’re entering the last three months of the calendar year, typically a strong quarter for semiconductor stocks, as seen in the chart below.

Takeaway

The observations that I make about semiconductor companies selling out their inventories at one of the fastest paces in the last 10 years are extremely telling for the bullish future of semiconductor companies that are part of SMH.

There is strong demand for chips, semiconductor components, and semiconductor equipment, which makes the SMH a strong candidate for portfolios.

I reiterate my Buy rating on SMH.
